title: Listening - Part 4: Early Retirement Experiences
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five individuals discussing their reasons for retiring early and the impact it had on them

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent decades in a high-pressure corporate environment, and [21]the constant stress was beginning to manifest as chronic health issues that I could no longer ignore[/21]. I decided to walk away at fifty-five. It was a terrifying leap, but [26]the immediate improvement in my physical well-being was nothing short of miraculous[/26]. I no longer wake up with a knot in my stomach. However, I hadn't anticipated how much I would miss the intellectual sparring with my colleagues. I’ve had to find new ways to keep my mind sharp, like joining a local debate society, which has been a surprisingly effective substitute for the boardroom. || Speaker 2: My husband and I had always been frugal, so [22]we reached our financial targets much earlier than expected and decided to enjoy our freedom while we were still active[/22]. We moved to the countryside to start an organic garden. It’s been wonderful, but [27]the most difficult adjustment was the sudden loss of a structured daily routine[/27]. Without deadlines, I found myself drifting through the weeks without achieving anything. I’ve had to become very disciplined about setting my own schedule to avoid a sense of lethargy. It’s a work in progress, but I’m finally finding a balance between relaxation and productivity. || Speaker 3: I was a senior partner in a law firm, but [23]I felt I’d become completely disconnected from my creative side over the years[/23]. I retired early to focus on landscape painting. It was a complete pivot. [28]What I find most rewarding now is the profound sense of peace I get from spending hours alone in nature[/28]. I never realized how much the noise of the city was affecting my mental clarity. I’m not making any money from my art, but that was never the point. I’ve regained a part of myself that I thought was lost forever, and that’s worth more than any salary. || Speaker 4: I’d reached the top of my profession, and [24]I simply felt that I’d achieved everything I wanted to and needed a fresh challenge outside of work[/24]. I retired at fifty to volunteer for an international charity. It’s been eye-opening. However, [29]I was surprised by the subtle sense of social isolation that comes when your professional network disappears[/29]. People you thought were friends suddenly stop calling when you’re no longer 'useful' to them. It’s been a tough lesson in the nature of professional relationships, but it’s helped me identify who my true friends really are. || Speaker 5: My decision was prompted by a sudden family bereavement. [25]It made me realize that life is far too short to spend forty hours a week doing something I didn't love[/25]. I quit my job in insurance the following month. [30]The most unexpected benefit has been the deep and meaningful connection I’ve built with my local community[/30]. I finally have time to help out at the neighborhood center and get to know the people living right next door. I used to just be a ghost in my own street, but now I feel like I truly belong somewhere. It’s a wonderful feeling of integration.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what prompted each speaker to retire early.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions chronic health issues as the main reason for walking away.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 says they reached their financial targets earlier than expected.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 felt disconnected from his creative side and wanted to focus on painting.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 felt she had achieved all her goals and needed a fresh challenge.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 mentions a bereavement made him realize life was too short to do work he didn't love.

options.D: the offer of a generous redundancy package
options.E: a significant change in their personal outlook
options.G: the desire to relocate to another country
options.H: a wish to prioritize artistic pursuits

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their retirement.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions an immediate and miraculous improvement in physical well-being.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 struggled with the sudden loss of a structured daily routine.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 finds a profound sense of peace and mental clarity in nature.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was surprised by the social isolation and the loss of her professional network.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the deep connection and sense of belonging in his community.

options.A: a renewed interest in academic study
options.B: an increased sense of inner tranquility
options.C: the discovery of a lucrative new hobby
options.D: a noticeable recovery in their health
options.E: the development of a closer family bond
options.F: the challenge of managing their own time
options.G: the realization that some friendships were superficial
options.H: a feeling of being integrated into the locality
